subject: Math
domain: Number and Operations in Base Ten

lesson.objective: Understand place value from millions to thousandths and powers of 10.
lesson.concept_explanation: Every place in a base-ten number is ten times the place to its right and one tenth of the place to its left. Decimals use tenths, hundredths, and thousandths to show parts of one whole.
lesson.common_misconception: A common mistake is thinking a longer decimal is always larger. Line up places and add zeros only as placeholders.
lesson.real_world_connection: Scientists, builders, and athletes use decimals to measure precisely.
lesson.lesson_summary: Use charts, grids, number lines, and rounding benchmarks to read, compare, shift, and round decimal values.

worked_examples.title: Read decimal places
worked_examples.text: In 6.482, the 4 is tenths, the 8 is hundredths, and the 2 is thousandths. So 6.482 is six and four hundred eighty-two thousandths.

worked_examples.title: Round a decimal
worked_examples.text: To round 3.46 to the nearest tenth, look at the hundredths digit. Since 6 is five or more, 3.46 rounds to 3.5.

hint_ladder: Line up the number in the place-value chart and name the place before answering.
For decimals, read the digit by its place: tenths, hundredths, or thousandths.
When multiplying or dividing by powers of 10, digits shift places while the decimal point marks the ones place.
When comparing or rounding decimals, write equivalent zeros so the places line up.

guided_practice.prompt: Which decimal is shown by four tenths?
hints: Line up the number in the place-value chart and name the place before answering.
For decimals, read the digit by its place: tenths, hundredths, or thousandths.
When multiplying or dividing by powers of 10, digits shift places while the decimal point marks the ones place.
When comparing or rounding decimals, write equivalent zeros so the places line up.

feedback.correct: Yes! You used place value to reason about decimals.
feedback.incorrect: Try again by lining up each digit in the correct place and reading the decimal places carefully.

guided_practice.explanation: The answer is 0.4 because each digit has a value based on its place.

misconception_bank.decimal_place_value_confusion: The student may be naming the wrong decimal place or overlooking zero placeholders.
misconception_bank.decimal_length_error: The student may think more digits after the decimal point always means a larger number.
misconception_bank.power_of_ten_shift_error: The student may move digits the wrong direction when multiplying or dividing by powers of ten.
misconception_bank.rounding_decimal_error: The student may round using the wrong place or midpoint.

game_theme.title: Decimal Observatory
game_theme.mission_intro: Help the observatory crew calibrate place-value lenses from millions to thousandths.

story.text: Welcome to the Decimal Observatory. Today you will help the crew label numbers from millions all the way to thousandths so every measurement lands in the correct place. Read the chart carefully, then press Start Mission when you are ready to begin.
story.label: Read This Page

lesson.text: Place value tells what each digit is worth. To the left, each place is ten times as large; to the right of the decimal point, places are tenths, hundredths, and thousandths. Press Next after you use the model to connect each digit with its value.
lesson.label: Read This Page

watch.text: Watch the tutor model decimal place value. Step one, place the decimal point after the ones place. Step two, read zero point three five as thirty-five hundredths and zero point one two five as one hundred twenty-five thousandths. Step three, compare or round by lining up places; press Next when the final answer makes sense.
watch.label: Read This Page

demo.text: Now try the guided demo with the place-value tools. Use the decimal grid, number line, and rounding model to explain what each digit means. Press Next when you can say the answer in words and point to the matching model.
demo.label: Read This Page

practice.text: Practice is your turn to answer with support. Tap Read Question to hear the decimal spoken naturally, use the hint if you need it, and choose or type the answer carefully. Press Check after you have lined up the places.
practice.label: Read This Page

challenge.text: Challenge problems mix place value, powers of ten, comparing, and rounding. Tap Read Question first so the decimal is read clearly, then use zeros as placeholders when needed. Press Check when your answer matches the value of the number.
challenge.label: Read This Page

checkpoint.text: Checkpoint shows what you can do independently. Tap Read Question, think about the decimal places from tenths to thousandths, and answer carefully. This helps decide whether you are ready for your badge.
checkpoint.label: Read This Page

badge.text: Great work, decimal explorer. You showed that whole-number places, decimal places, and powers of ten all fit in one base-ten system. Press Next to save your badge and review your growth.
badge.label: Read This Page

profile.text: Your profile records this Skill World mission and opens Skill Practice for more fluency. Use the Practice This Skill path when you want more questions by level. You can also go back to the hub to choose another mission and keep building your Grade 5 math map.
profile.label: Read This Page
